medications.indication_rationale: First-line for cutaneous lichen planus; reduces inflammation and itch.
Helps break the nocturnal itch-scratch cycle and restore sleep, short-term.
medications.dose_route_frequency: per standard adult label dosing at night

medications.indication_rationale: Not indicated — this is not scabies.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep nails short, wear long-sleeved cotton at night over the treated skin, avoid hot showers/harsh soaps.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Breaks the itch-scratch cycle and protects the skin.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like lichen planus — an autoimmune, non-contagious itchy rash. You don't need to treat your house for scabies.
Use the steroid ointment on the bumps and a night antihistamine to control the itch and sleep; keep nails short and cover the skin at night.

See your GP within a week to confirm (possibly a small biopsy). See a doctor sooner if the scratched areas become hot, spreading red, or ooze pus, or you get a fever, or the rash spreads to your mouth or genitals.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Confirm diagnosis, response to topical therapy, screen for oral/genital/nail involvement.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Secondary infection signs, or mucosal spread.

must_include_items: Reassure non-contagious based on morphology/Wickham striae/absent burrows/contacts
Potent topical corticosteroid ± night antihistamine for itch/sleep
errors_of_commission: Escalating to ED for a benign itchy rash
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: T2 case: passing requires non-contagion reassurance + itch relief + in-person confirmation. Household scabicide or ED escalation is a failure.
